title: Nonreciprocal Dispersive Coupling for Quantum Sensing
abstract: Dispersive coupling is widely utilized for quantum information readout. Most prior studies have concentrated on reciprocal dispersive coupling. Here, we further construct nonreciprocal dispersive coupling and apply it to quantum sensing. For cavity photon number measurement, nonreciprocal dispersive coupling delivers higher precision than its reciprocal counterpart, and this advantage grows more pronounced with an increase in photon number. When directly measuring the single-photon driving strength, however, nonreciprocal dispersive coupling shows no superiority over reciprocal dispersive coupling. By converting the information of driving strength into cavity photon numbers via our proposed strategy, nonreciprocal dispersive coupling again outperforms reciprocal dispersive coupling in precision, with the advantage becoming increasingly significant at larger driving strength. This work presents a novel method to boost quantum sensing and enable the fabrication of ultra-precise quantum sensors.
summary: This paper theoretically investigates how nonreciprocal dispersive coupling can enhance quantum sensing beyond what is achievable with standard reciprocal coupling. By analyzing the measurement uncertainty using the Quantum Fisher Information, the authors demonstrate that nonreciprocity provides an exponential advantage in precision for measuring both photon number and driving strength. This work proposes a novel method to build ultra-precise quantum sensors using engineered nonreciprocal interactions.
main_problem: The paper compares the quantum sensing capabilities of nonreciprocal versus reciprocal dispersive coupling in a qubit-cavity system.
main_result: Nonreciprocal coupling provides superior measurement precision for both cavity photon number and driving strength compared to its reciprocal counterpart, especially at high photon numbers or driving strengths.
method: The authors derived measurement uncertainties using the Quantum Fisher Information and the Quantum Cramér-Rao bound for both coupling types.
model_or_system: The system involves a qubit coupled dispersively to a cavity mode, which is modeled using a Hamiltonian and analyzed via the master equation and quantum Langevin equations.
key_observables: Measurement uncertainty ($\delta^2 n$ for photon number; $\delta^2 \epsilon$ for driving strength $\epsilon$), and Quantum Fisher Information ($F_Q$).
important_parameters: Dispersive coupling strengths ($\lambda$, $\Gamma$), cavity dissipation rate ($\kappa$), initial photon number ($n$), and driving strength ($\epsilon$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on adiabatic elimination of an intermediate bosonic mode and assumes specific dissipation rate hierarchies.
figures_summary: Figure 1 schematically illustrates the nonreciprocal coupling setup and its equivalent form. Figure 2 (mentioned) likely compares the ratio of measurement uncertainties ($\delta_{nr}/\delta_{nnr}$) versus photon number $n$.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by establishing the Hamiltonian for both coupling types, deriving the evolution equations, calculating the Quantum Fisher Information for both, and finally comparing the resulting measurement uncertainties for photon number and driving strength using novel measurement strategies.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to AMO physics and quantum optics as it provides a theoretical framework for enhancing quantum measurement precision by engineering nonreciprocal interactions in cavity QED systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Schematic diagram of nonreciprocal dispersive cou- pling. (A) The cavity mode a is coupled to the bosonic mode b with the coupling strength denoted by g. The qubit couples to the cavity mode a and bosonic b, with dispersive coupling strengths λ and χ, respectively. The dissipation rate suﬀered by the bosonic mode b is much higher than that of the cavity mode a, κb ≫(κ, g). (B) Schematic diagram of the equivalent nonreciprocal coupling between the cavity and the qubit. Af- ter fully eliminating bosonic mode b adiabatically, the cavity mode a couples to the qubit σz via nonreciprocal dispersive coupling with strength Γ.
Figure 2. Ratio of measurement uncertainty δnr/δnnr versus cavity photon number n and angle θ under reciprocal and nonreciprocal dispersive coupling. Here, we consider that λ ≫ κ and Γ ≫κ.
